title: Who Are G7’s Top Performers?
gist: CSIS's Victor Cha, ahead of the June 15-17 2026 G7 summit at Evian-les-Bains, presents data-driven performance rankings showing the G7's shrinking global weight (44.1% of GDP, 9.6% of population vs 60.5%/13.8% in 1980) and argues for expanding to a 'G9' including Australia and South Korea, which outperform several G7 members on the bloc's own priority issues. The summit agenda centers on Iran/Hormuz energy disruption, Ukraine, AI (EU data-center rules, US AI-stack exports), and critical minerals.

insights.claim: The G7's share of world GDP fell from 60.5% in 1980 to 44.1% today and its population share from 13.8% to 9.6%, even as its agenda globalized to cover AI, development, and managing China.
insights.speaker: Victor Cha
insights.so_what: Quantifies the legitimacy gap driving G7 reform debate; a shrinking bloc tackling an expanding agenda underpins the expansion case.
insights.quote: In 1980, the G7 accounted for 60.5 percent of world GDP and 13.8 percent of the world's population. Today, it accounts for 44.1 percent of world GDP and 9.6 percent of the global population.
insights.locator: The G7 Is Not Representative as a Global Governance Institution

insights.claim: The 2026 G7 is the first leaders' meeting since US-Israeli action on Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz, making it a 'back to the future' moment echoing the 1973 G7 finance-ministers convening after the first oil shock.
insights.speaker: Victor Cha
insights.so_what: Frames the summit as oil-shock crisis coordination; watch for G7 macro/energy policy signals tied to Hormuz.
insights.quote: The G7 meeting of financial ministers was first convened in 1973 in response to the first oil shock.
insights.locator: In one sense, this year's G7 meeting represents a “back to the future” moment

insights.claim: G7 leaders will seek to coordinate on AI—including EU regulation of data centers' environmental and energy impact and US exporting of the 'American AI stack'—and on securing critical-mineral supply chains.
insights.speaker: Victor Cha
insights.so_what: Signals EU data-center energy rules and US AI-stack export policy as live G7 agenda items—relevant to AI-infrastructure and minerals positioning.
insights.quote: EU regulations on the environmental and energy impact of data centers and the United States' exporting of the American AI stack
insights.locator: The G7 leaders will seek to coordinate policies on AI

themes.theme: Performance rankings and the G9 case

insights.claim: Using 347 performance metrics across seven priority issues, CSIS ranks Germany, the UK, and the US as the top three G7 performers overall, with the US strongest on Ukraine and digital science/technology and Germany on climate and economic resilience.
insights.speaker: Victor Cha
insights.so_what: Provides an evidence base for which G7 members lead on which issues—useful for anticipating who drives policy in each domain.
insights.quote: the top three performers overall are Germany, the United Kingdom, and the United States.
insights.locator: G7 Performance Ranking on Selected Issues

insights.claim: Australia and South Korea outperform several G7 members on the bloc's priority issues (South Korea ranks second only to the US on digital science/technology and outperforms Italy across all issues), making them strong candidates for a 'G9'.
insights.speaker: Victor Cha
insights.so_what: Cha's G9 thesis flags Australia and South Korea as the most credible expansion candidates—a structural shift in global-governance weight to watch.
insights.quote: two countries—Australia and South Korea—are strong candidates for inclusion in a G9 configuration.
insights.locator: The Case for Expanding the G7 to the G9

insights.claim: India and Brazil, despite being important G7 liaison partners, rank below the lowest-performing G7 member on every priority issue except climate—undercutting their case for inclusion on a performance basis.
insights.speaker: Victor Cha
insights.so_what: Counters the conventional expectation that India/Brazil are next for G-club expansion; performance data favors Australia/Korea instead.
insights.quote: They both ranked below the lowest performing G7 member on every issue area except for climate
insights.locator: It is also noteworthy that other notable geopolitical actors...
